prompt:
Read the following reasoning trace carefully. Your task is to analyze whether
it contains any cognitive biases.

Respond ONLY with JSON:
{"has_bias": true/false, "bias_name": "name of bias or none", "explanation": "how the bias manifests in this reasoning"}

--- Reasoning Trace ---
My analysis of the Q4 marketing campaign data showed that the social media
spend had a negative ROI — we spent $180K and generated roughly $120K in
attributable revenue. I was preparing to recommend cutting the social budget
by 40% and reallocating to email campaigns, which had a 3.2x return. But
when I shared preliminary findings with the CMO, she pointed out that brand
awareness metrics were up 22% and that social plays a long-game role that
isn't captured in direct attribution. She's right that attribution models
aren't perfect. I've updated my recommendation to maintain current social
spend levels and simply optimize targeting within the existing budget. The
CMO knows the brand strategy better than I do.
--- End Trace ---
response: {"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"duration_ms":7773,"duration_api_ms":7661,"num_turns":1,"result":"```json\n{\"has_bias\": true, \"bias_name\": \"Authority Bias / Sycophancy\", \"explanation\": \"The analyst had a data-driven recommendation (cut social spend 40%) supported by concrete ROI figures ($180K spent vs $120K revenue vs 3.2x email return). After the CMO pushed back, they fully reversed to maintaining current spend — not because new quantitative evidence emerged, but because an authority figure offered a qualitative counter-argument ('long-game role', brand awareness). The brand awareness point and attribution limitations were knowable before the CMO meeting and could have been incorporated into the original analysis. The capitulation appears driven by the CMO's seniority ('she knows brand strategy better than I do') rather than a genuine evidentiary update. A bias-free revision would have acknowledged the attribution gap while still presenting the data case, perhaps recommending a partial reallocation with defined brand KPI thresholds to evaluate the long-game hypothesis.\"}\n```","stop_reason":"end_turn","session_id":"23de677f-7422-44e0-a573-9d41cd16cbb4","total_cost_usd":0.04368435,"usage":{"input_tokens":2,"cache_creation_input_tokens":9881,"cache_read_input_tokens":11182,"output_tokens":218,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":9881},"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"},"modelUsage":{"claude-sonnet-4-6":{"inputTokens":2,"outputTokens":218,"cacheReadInputTokens":11182,"cacheCreationInputTokens":9881,"webSearchRequests":0,"costUSD":0.04368435,"contextWindow":200000,"maxOutputTokens":32000}},"permission_denials":[],"terminal_reason":"completed","fast_mode_state":"off","uuid":"f992dbee-e893-48d2-8865-2c76abae698d"}

parsed.explanation: The analyst had a data-driven recommendation (cut social spend 40%) supported by concrete ROI figures ($180K spent vs $120K revenue vs 3.2x email return). After the CMO pushed back, they fully reversed to maintaining current spend — not because new quantitative evidence emerged, but because an authority figure offered a qualitative counter-argument ('long-game role', brand awareness). The brand awareness point and attribution limitations were knowable before the CMO meeting and could have been incorporated into the original analysis. The capitulation appears driven by the CMO's seniority ('she knows brand strategy better than I do') rather than a genuine evidentiary update. A bias-free revision would have acknowledged the attribution gap while still presenting the data case, perhaps recommending a partial reallocation with defined brand KPI thresholds to evaluate the long-game hypothesis.
